Policymakers and financial institutions must remain agile as global dynamics shift.

items.title: Quote of the day by Robert Wilson: "The only way one makes money in the market is when the market’s perception of a stock changes"
items.summary: Stock prices are driven by shifts in market perception, not just current performance. Investors make money when expectations change, moving from pessimism to optimism or recognizing overlooked value. Identifying these potential shifts early is crucial for significant investment gains as markets look ahead.

This aims to lessen dependence on the Strait of Hormuz amid West Asia tensions. The nation must prepare for economic shocks by building strategic reserves and shifting towards green energy.

items.title: SC upholds govt retrospective 28% GST levy on online gaming companies

The court ruled that online gaming platforms are not intermediaries and their activities are taxable. This decision impacts past transactions and tax demands. Online gaming companies had challenged the retrospective tax, arguing skill-based games are not gambling. The government's interpretation now applies to these past events.

items.title: Watch: Whatever happened to India’s ambitious biogas plans?

items.summary: The Aurangabad-based company aims to mop up nearly Rs 1,162 crore (at higher end of price band) by diluting 17.5 percent stake through the issue that will close on October 7. It already raised Rs 348.52 crore through anchor investors' portion on Tuesday, the day before issue opening.

items.title: Cautiously optimistic on India; eyeing earnings revival: Sanger
items.summary: Global liquidity tailwinds are unlikely to get any better from now and that makes Arvind Sanger, Managing Partner at Geosphere Capital Management “cautiously optimistic” on the Indian markets.

items.title: Automation threatens 69% jobs in India: World Bank
items.summary: "In large parts of Africa, it is likely that technology could fundamentally disrupt this pattern. Research based on World Bank data has predicted that the proportion of jobs threatened in India by automation is 69 percent, in China it is 77 percent and in Ethiopia, the percentage of jobs threatened by automation is 85 percent," he said.
